title: Incident Handling
controlText: Establish an operational incident-handling capability for organizational systems that includes preparation, detection, analysis, containment, recovery, and user response activities.
plainEnglish: Have a working incident response capability — not just a plan document, but the people, tools, contacts, processes, and trained-up roles to actually execute when something goes wrong. The six named components (preparation, detection, analysis, containment, recovery, user response) are each assessed independently. A mature IR capability feeds directly into AU log requirements, SI monitoring, and CM change tracking. An organization with no functioning IR capability is exposed on every other control that depends on detection and response to work.

objectives.text: an operational incident-handling capability is established
objectives.whatToLookFor: An incident response capability that is actually operational — not just documented. Evidence includes an Incident Response Plan, named IR team roles or responsibilities, a ticketing or tracking mechanism for incidents, and contact lists for internal and external notification. 'Operational' means the people know their roles and have the tools to execute them, not just that a plan document exists.
commonEvidence: Incident Response Policy and Plan
IR team roster with roles and responsibilities
Incident tracking system configuration (ticketing tool, SIEM case management)
IR contact list including internal stakeholders and external reporting authorities
System Security Plan section on IR capability

objectives.text: the operational incident-handling capability includes preparation
objectives.whatToLookFor: Preparation activities — IR training records, pre-positioned tools (forensic kits, offline backups, clean images), documented playbooks, and evidence that personnel have completed IR training. Preparation is the phase that happens before an incident occurs.
commonEvidence: IR training records for personnel with response responsibilities
Pre-defined IR playbooks or runbooks
Forensic and investigation tool inventory
Communication templates and escalation procedures

objectives.text: the operational incident-handling capability includes detection
objectives.whatToLookFor: Detection mechanisms that alert on potential incidents — SIEM alerts, AV/EDR detections, IDS/IPS events, anomalous behavior alerts. The capability should identify how potential incidents reach the IR team: who receives alerts, what thresholds trigger escalation.
commonEvidence: SIEM alert rules and detection coverage documentation
EDR/AV alert configuration and escalation thresholds
IDS/IPS event routing to IR team
Documented incident indicator categories

objectives.text: the operational incident-handling capability includes analysis
objectives.whatToLookFor: A defined analysis process — how potential incidents are triaged, what information is collected, who performs analysis, and how severity is determined. Evidence includes incident ticket records showing analysis documented, analyst notes, or SIEM investigation workflows.
commonEvidence: Incident triage procedures
Sample incident tickets showing analysis documentation
Severity classification criteria
SIEM investigation workflow or SOC runbook

objectives.text: the operational incident-handling capability includes containment
objectives.whatToLookFor: Defined containment actions and evidence they are used — network isolation procedures, account suspension procedures, block/quarantine capability in EDR, documented containment decision authority. Sample incident records showing containment actions taken.
commonEvidence: Containment procedures in the IR plan
Sample incident records documenting containment actions
Network isolation runbook (VLAN segregation, firewall rule emergency procedures)
EDR isolation/quarantine capability

objectives.text: the operational incident-handling capability includes recovery
objectives.whatToLookFor: Recovery procedures — system restoration steps, backup restoration procedures, evidence of eradication before recovery, post-incident validation. Sample incident records showing recovery activities documented.
commonEvidence: Recovery procedures in the IR plan
Backup and restore procedures
Sample incident records showing recovery phase
Post-recovery validation checklists

objectives.text: the operational incident-handling capability includes user response activities
objectives.whatToLookFor: User-facing components of IR — training for non-security users on how to recognize and report incidents, help desk procedures for receiving incident reports, awareness program elements on suspicious activity. Users are often the first detection layer.
commonEvidence: Security awareness training content covering incident reporting
Incident reporting mechanism communicated to all users (email alias, help desk number)
Help desk procedure for receiving and routing potential incident reports
User incident reporting records or tickets

commonArtifacts: Incident Response Policy and Plan
IR team roles and responsibilities documentation
Incident tracking system (ticketing tool or SIEM case management)
IR playbooks and runbooks per incident type
IR training records
Sample incident tickets showing the full lifecycle
Contact lists for internal officials and external reporting authorities
After-action / lessons learned reports from prior incidents or exercises
commonGaps: IR plan exists as a document but no one has read it or been trained on it in the past year
No incident tracking system — incidents managed via email threads with no central record
Detection capability limited to AV alerts; no SIEM or correlation across log sources
Containment procedures not defined — responders improvise under pressure
Recovery procedures not documented or tested; reliance on institutional knowledge
Users have no way to report suspicious activity; incidents only discovered by IT
IR plan does not name specific internal officials or external authorities for notification
Incident response roles assigned to individuals who have since left the organization

title: Incident Reporting
controlText: Track, document, and report incidents to designated officials and/or authorities both internal and external to the organization.
plainEnglish: When incidents happen, they need to be tracked in a system (not just in someone's memory), documented with enough detail to support investigation and post-incident review, and reported — both to internal leadership and to external authorities where required. For defense contractors, this typically means reporting to DCSA and/or US-CERT within the timeframe required by the DFARS 252.204-7012 clause (72 hours for cyber incidents involving CUI). The Assessment Guide notes six distinct objectives that assessors verify independently: tracking, documentation, identifying internal officials, identifying external authorities, notifying authorities, notifying internal officials.

objectives.whatToLookFor: A system for tracking incidents from initial report through closure — a ticketing tool, SIEM case management, or equivalent. Assessors look for evidence that open incidents have a status and owner, and closed incidents have documented closure reasons.
commonEvidence: Incident tracking system with open and closed incident records
Incident ticket lifecycle workflow
SIEM case management configuration

objectives.whatToLookFor: Incident records contain enough detail to reconstruct what happened — timeline, affected systems, indicators of compromise, actions taken, and resolution. Documentation quality should be consistent, not dependent on individual analyst thoroughness.
commonEvidence: Sample incident tickets showing complete documentation
Incident documentation template or required fields
After-action reports for significant incidents

objectives.text: authorities to whom incidents are to be reported are identified
objectives.whatToLookFor: External reporting authorities explicitly named in IR policy or plan — for CMMC contractors this typically means US-CERT (CISA), DCSA, and potentially DoD Cyber Crime Center (DC3). Timeframes for reporting should be specified (DFARS 252.204-7012 requires 72 hours).
commonEvidence: IR plan section naming external reporting authorities
DFARS 252.204-7012 clause in contracts
Documented reporting timeframes and thresholds

objectives.text: organizational officials to whom incidents are to be reported are identified
objectives.whatToLookFor: Named internal stakeholders who must be notified — typically CISO, CIO, legal counsel, executive leadership, and affected business unit owners. Contact information current and role-based rather than individual-only.
commonEvidence: IR plan section naming internal reporting officials by role
Escalation matrix with contact information
Incident notification procedure

objectives.text: identified authorities are notified of incidents
objectives.whatToLookFor: Evidence that external authorities actually receive notification when required — submitted DFARS cyber incident reports, US-CERT report confirmation records, or documented decisions that a specific incident did not meet the reporting threshold with the reasoning.
commonEvidence: DFARS 252.204-7012 incident report submissions
US-CERT report confirmation receipts
Incident records documenting external notification actions or threshold-not-met determination

objectives.text: identified organizational officials are notified of incidents
objectives.whatToLookFor: Evidence that internal stakeholders were actually notified — email threads, meeting notes, executive status reports tied to specific incidents. Pattern of internal notification visible across multiple incidents.
commonEvidence: Sample incident records showing internal notification actions
Executive status reports tied to incidents
Escalation notification records in ticketing system

commonArtifacts: Incident Response Policy and Plan
Incident tracking system with historical records
Incident documentation templates
Escalation matrix (internal officials) with current contact information
External reporting authority contacts and DFARS 252.204-7012 documentation
Sample incident tickets showing tracking, documentation, and notification
DFARS cyber incident report submissions
After-action reports
commonGaps: DFARS 252.204-7012 reporting requirement not known by IR team — external reporting obligation unmet
Incidents tracked in email or spreadsheets with no consistent fields or lifecycle workflow
Internal reporting officials not named by role — named by individual, causing gaps when personnel change
No documentation of threshold-not-met decisions — absence of external reports creates ambiguity
Incident records contain only technical details; business impact and management notification not documented
Reporting chain only goes to IT management; executive leadership and legal not included
Incident tickets closed without documenting resolution — cannot reconstruct what happened post-closure

title: Incident Response Testing
controlText: Test the organizational incident response capability.
plainEnglish: The IR plan and capability must be tested, not just documented. Testing validates that processes actually work, that people know their roles, and that gaps are identified before a real incident exposes them. The Assessment Guide explicitly notes that testing methods include tabletop exercises, walkthroughs, simulations, and comprehensive exercises. After-action reports documenting what was found and what was improved are the primary evidence. The frequency is not prescribed by the control itself — the Assessment Guide points to organizational risk tolerance — but annual testing is the most common baseline, with more frequent testing for organizations in high-threat sectors.

objectives.text: the incident response capability is tested
objectives.whatToLookFor: Evidence that IR testing actually occurred within a recent period — tabletop exercise records, simulation results, or full-interrupt exercise documentation. The test should be planned, executed with relevant personnel, and documented with findings and improvements. A test that produces no findings is suspicious; the exercise should surface something.
commonEvidence: IR tabletop exercise plans and execution records
After-action report from the most recent IR test
Lessons learned / improvement items with owners and status
Participant list confirming relevant roles were included
Updated IR plan showing revisions triggered by test findings

commonArtifacts: Incident response test plan
Tabletop exercise scenario and facilitator guide
After-action report with findings and improvement items
Updated IR plan incorporating lessons learned
Participant attendance records
commonGaps: IR plan has never been tested since it was written
Testing only done when prompted by an audit — not on a regular organizational schedule
Tabletop exercise completed but no after-action report produced and no improvements tracked
Exercise participants are only IT security staff; legal, HR, communications, and executive roles not included
Test findings identified but IR plan not updated — lessons learned document created but disconnected from the plan
Organization treats a real incident as the 'test' — no proactive exercise conducted
